hub_subtitle: From hesitant communicator to confident global business leader

lesson.menu_desc: The investor said yes in principle — now the deal has to survive due diligence and the fine print. Milton sits down with Diana to disclose the numbers, agree the terms and sign. Learn the conditional connectors that set the rules of any agreement — provided that, as long as, unless, on condition that — alongside the language of the close: due diligence, a clause, an audit, compliance, to waive, to undertake, a binding agreement — and walk out with a deal you understand and can stand behind — 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 19 — Closing the Investment
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Milton Sayegh | Aula 19 — Closing the Investment | Business English for Luxury Export | Alumni by Better

listenings.text: Hi Milton, it's Diana. Good news first: I'm in. I loved the pitch, and the numbers hold up, so we are moving to closing. Before I wire anything, my team needs to run due diligence, which is normal, please don't read anything into it. I'll send a list, but the main things are simple. I need to see audited accounts for the last two years, your supplier contracts, and confirmation that you're fully compliant with export rules. As long as those check out, we can sign quickly. One condition from my side: the agreement will include a clause that you won't take on a competing backer for the first year. That's standard, and I'm happy to explain it. Unless something strange turns up, I'd like to have a binding agreement signed within three weeks. Send me the documents whenever you're ready, and call me if any term feels unfair. I'd rather fix it now than after we sign. Talk soon.

Hey, it's Milton, just a quick note to myself the day we signed. A year ago I could barely get through a phone call in English, and today I read every clause of an investment agreement, negotiated two of them, and signed. Diana's team ran due diligence, they wanted audited accounts and proof of compliance, and because everything was clean, it went fast. I didn't just nod along this time. When one clause felt too tight, I asked her to change it, and I said we could agree provided that the non-compete was limited to one year. She said yes. I undertook to keep the records open for an annual audit, and she agreed to waive the early penalty as long as we hit the first milestone. The deal is binding now, and the strange thing is I'm not nervous. I understood every word I signed. That is the difference a year makes. The money matters, but reading that contract and holding my own in the negotiation, that is the real win.
